markers.name: false_analogy
markers.excerpt: there was two holocausts in America... the holocaustic aspects, the Auschwitzian aspects of the slave trade
markers.explanation: Tarantino draws a direct equivalence between slavery and the Holocaust/Auschwitz. While both involve mass atrocity, conflating distinct historical events as analogous can be a rhetorical false analogy used to dramatize a point.

markers.name: loaded_language
markers.excerpt: the holocaustic aspects, the Auschwitzian aspects of the slave trade in America
markers.explanation: Using emotionally charged terms like 'holocaustic' and 'Auschwitzian' to frame the subject matter loads the discussion with strong affective weight beyond descriptive accuracy.

markers.name: red_herring
markers.excerpt: you go and you watch a Kung-Fu movie and one guy takes on a hundred people in a restaurant
markers.explanation: When asked why non-violent people enjoy movie violence, Tarantino pivots to a kung-fu movie example rather than addressing the psychological question, deflecting from the substance of the inquiry.

markers.name: false_analogy
markers.excerpt: filmmaking directors are like boxers. They have their time.
markers.explanation: Tarantino compares directors to boxers to argue both decline with age. While evocative, the analogy equates physical athletic decline with creative/artistic decline, which are not obviously comparable domains.
